d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which BEST describes the relationship between AI, machine learning, and deep learning?
o: AI is a subset of machine learning, which uses neural networks to process data
Machine learning is a subset of AI, and deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses neural networks
Deep learning contains both AI and machine learning as specialized subcategories
AI and machine learning are separate disciplines that deep learning combines into one framework
e: Correct (B): AI is the broadest field, ML is a technique within AI that learns from data, and deep learning is a specialized ML approach using multi-layer neural networks. A is wrong: AI is the superset, not the subset of ML - the hierarchy is reversed. C is wrong: Deep learning is the most specific/narrow category, not the broadest - it cannot contain AI and ML. D is wrong: ML is explicitly a subset of AI, not a separate discipline - they are nested, not parallel.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A company wants to predict which customers will cancel their subscription next month based on usage patterns. Which type of ML problem is this?
o: Unsupervised clustering that groups customers by similarity without predefined labels
Supervised classification that predicts a categorical outcome from labeled historical data
Reinforcement learning where an agent maximizes reward through trial-and-error interactions
Generative modeling that creates synthetic customer profiles for testing purposes
e: Correct (B): Predicting churn (cancel/not cancel) is a binary classification task using labeled historical data (past customers who did/didn't cancel). A is wrong: Clustering finds groups without labels, but churn prediction needs labeled outcomes (churned vs retained) to train on. C is wrong: Reinforcement learning optimizes sequential decisions through rewards, not one-time predictions from historical data. D is wrong: Generative modeling creates new data samples, it doesn't predict categorical outcomes for existing customers.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which TWO scenarios indicate AI/ML is NOT appropriate? (Select TWO)
o: Classifying support tickets into categories based on historical resolution patterns
Performing a fixed regulatory compliance calculation that must produce identical results every time
Generating personalized product recommendations based on browsing and purchase history
Sorting a database table alphabetically by a single column field
Predicting equipment failure probability from sensor telemetry and maintenance logs
e: Correct (B, D): B is a deterministic calculation requiring identical, auditable outputs every time - ML adds unpredictability where none is acceptable. D is a basic algorithmic operation (alphabetical sort) requiring zero pattern recognition. A is wrong: Ticket classification benefits from ML pattern matching across unstructured text. C is wrong: Recommendations require learning complex user preference patterns. E is wrong: Predictive maintenance is a classic ML use case finding failure patterns in sensor data.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A retail company wants to discover natural groupings in customer purchase behavior without predefined categories. Which approach?
o: Supervised regression that predicts continuous purchase amounts from demographic features
Unsupervised clustering that identifies natural patterns and segments without labeled training data
Supervised classification that assigns customers to predefined loyalty tier categories
Reinforcement learning that optimizes product placement through sequential store layout decisions
e: Correct (B): Discovering natural groupings without predefined categories is the definition of unsupervised clustering (e.g., K-means, DBSCAN). A is wrong: Regression predicts a number, and supervised methods need labels - the scenario has no predefined target variable. C is wrong: Classification assigns to predefined categories, but the scenario explicitly wants to discover groupings without predefined categories. D is wrong: RL optimizes sequential decisions through rewards, not customer segmentation from purchase data.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A data science team has a dataset with 50 features. They want to reduce dimensionality while preserving variance. Which technique?
o: Principal Component Analysis that projects data onto lower-dimensional space preserving maximum variance
Gradient boosting that combines weak learners sequentially to minimize prediction error
Logistic regression that maps features to probability of binary classification outcomes
Convolutional neural network that extracts spatial features through learnable filter operations
e: Correct (A): PCA is a dimensionality reduction technique that finds orthogonal components capturing maximum variance, projecting to fewer dimensions. B is wrong: Gradient boosting is a supervised ensemble method for prediction, not dimensionality reduction. C is wrong: Logistic regression is a classification algorithm - it uses features, it doesn't reduce them. D is wrong: CNNs are deep learning architectures for spatial data (images), not general-purpose dimensionality reduction tools.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: During the ML development lifecycle, what happens in the feature engineering phase?
o: The model architecture is selected and hyperparameters are tuned for optimal performance
Raw data is transformed into meaningful input variables that improve model predictive power
The trained model is deployed to a production endpoint for real-time inference serving
Business stakeholders define success metrics and acceptance criteria for the project
e: Correct (B): Feature engineering transforms raw data into useful inputs (scaling, encoding, combining, deriving new features) that help the model find patterns. A is wrong: Model selection and hyperparameter tuning happen after features are prepared - this is the modeling phase. C is wrong: Deployment to production happens after training and evaluation - this is the deployment phase. D is wrong: Defining success metrics happens during problem framing at the project's start, before any data work.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A model performs excellently on training data but poorly on new unseen data. What is this called?
o: Underfitting, where the model is too simple to capture underlying patterns in the data
Overfitting, where the model memorized training data noise instead of learning generalizable patterns
Data drift, where the production data distribution has shifted from what the model was trained on
Feature leakage, where future information accidentally included during training inflates metrics
e: Correct (B): High training accuracy but low test/production accuracy is the classic definition of overfitting - the model memorized noise. A is wrong: Underfitting shows poor performance on BOTH training and test data because the model is too simple. C is wrong: Data drift occurs in production over time, not immediately during evaluation on a held-out test set. D is wrong: Feature leakage inflates BOTH training and test metrics because the leaked info is present in both datasets.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which metric is MOST appropriate for evaluating a fraud detection model where fraudulent transactions are very rare?
o: Overall accuracy that measures the percentage of all predictions that were correct
Precision-recall and F1 score that account for performance on the minority class specifically
Mean squared error that measures average squared difference between predicted and actual values
R-squared that measures the proportion of variance in the target explained by the model
e: Correct (B): With imbalanced classes (rare fraud), accuracy is misleading (99% accurate by predicting all legitimate). Precision-recall focuses on the minority class performance. A is wrong: Accuracy would show 99.9% just by predicting everything as legitimate - useless for fraud detection. C is wrong: MSE is a regression metric for continuous values, not classification of categorical fraud/not-fraud. D is wrong: R-squared measures variance explained in continuous regression, not classification performance.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is the purpose of splitting data into training, validation, and test sets?
o: Training set is for feature engineering, validation for model training, test for hyperparameter tuning
Training set builds the model, validation tunes hyperparameters, test provides final unbiased evaluation
Training set is for large models, validation for medium models, test for small production models
All three sets are trained simultaneously to ensure the model sees maximum data volume
e: Correct (B): Training teaches the model, validation helps select hyperparameters/architecture without touching test data, test gives final unbiased generalization estimate. A is wrong: Feature engineering happens before splitting; training set is for learning, not feature engineering. C is wrong: The split isn't about model size - all three sets use the same model, evaluated at different stages. D is wrong: Only training data is used to fit the model; validation and test are held out specifically to evaluate.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A company needs to predict the exact dollar amount of next month's revenue. Which type of ML task?
o: Supervised regression that predicts a continuous numerical output from input features
Supervised classification that assigns revenue into predefined high/medium/low categories
Unsupervised anomaly detection that identifies unusual revenue patterns without labels
Reinforcement learning that optimizes pricing decisions to maximize cumulative revenue
e: Correct (A): Predicting an exact dollar amount is a continuous value prediction, which is regression (e.g., linear regression, XGBoost regressor). B is wrong: Classification predicts categories, not exact dollar amounts - discretizing revenue into buckets loses precision. C is wrong: Anomaly detection flags outliers, it doesn't predict future values. D is wrong: RL optimizes sequential decisions, it doesn't directly forecast a specific future number from historical data.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which AWS service provides a fully managed environment for building, training, and deploying ML models?
o: Amazon Bedrock which provides access to foundation models through a unified API interface
Amazon SageMaker AI which offers end-to-end ML workflow from data preparation through deployment
AWS Lambda which runs serverless functions triggered by events without managing infrastructure
Amazon Comprehend which provides pre-built NLP capabilities for text analysis tasks
e: Correct (B): SageMaker AI is the comprehensive ML platform covering notebooks, training, tuning, and deployment. A is wrong: Bedrock provides access to pre-built foundation models, not a full custom ML build/train/deploy workflow. C is wrong: Lambda runs code in response to events - it's compute, not an ML development platform. D is wrong: Comprehend is a pre-built NLP API - you consume it, you don't build or train custom models with it.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A healthcare company needs to analyze medical images for tumors but has only 200 labeled images. Which technique helps most?
o: Training a deep neural network from scratch using only the 200 available labeled images
Transfer learning using a pre-trained model fine-tuned on the 200 domain-specific labeled images
Unsupervised clustering to group images by visual similarity without using any labels
Increasing the learning rate to force the model to learn faster from limited available data
e: Correct (B): Transfer learning uses a model pre-trained on millions of images (e.g., ImageNet), then fine-tunes on small domain data. Requires far less labeled data. A is wrong: 200 images is far too few to train a deep network from scratch - it will massively overfit. C is wrong: Clustering doesn't use labels and won't identify tumors specifically - it just groups by visual similarity. D is wrong: High learning rate with limited data causes unstable training and worse performance, not better learning.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is the key difference between batch inference and real-time inference?
o: Batch inference uses GPUs while real-time inference only uses CPUs for cost optimization
Batch inference processes large volumes of data on a schedule, real-time serves individual predictions on demand with low latency
Batch inference is more accurate because it processes more data simultaneously per request
Real-time inference trains the model continuously while batch inference uses a static model snapshot
e: Correct (B): Batch processes accumulated data periodically (e.g., nightly recommendations). Real-time returns predictions immediately per request (e.g., fraud check at checkout). A is wrong: Both can use GPUs or CPUs depending on workload - hardware choice isn't what defines them. C is wrong: Both use the same model and produce the same predictions - batch isn't inherently more accurate. D is wrong: Neither retrains during inference - both use a pre-trained model; the difference is timing and volume.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A model's predictions systematically favor one demographic group over another. What type of problem is this?
o: Model overfitting due to excessive training epochs on imbalanced demographic data
Bias in the ML system resulting from imbalanced training data or flawed feature selection
Data drift where the production population demographics shifted after initial model training
High variance in the model causing inconsistent predictions across random data samples
e: Correct (B): Systematic favoritism of one group is bias, caused by imbalanced representation, biased labels, or features that proxy for protected attributes. A is wrong: Overfitting means poor generalization across all data, not systematic group-level favoritism. C is wrong: Drift is temporal change in data distribution, not built-in systematic group favoritism present from training. D is wrong: High variance means different predictions from different training samples - it's random instability, not systematic group-level preference.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which describes the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
o: Supervised learning requires human-labeled target variables while unsupervised finds patterns without labels
Supervised learning uses neural networks while unsupervised learning only uses statistical methods
Supervised learning is for production systems while unsupervised learning is only for research purposes
Supervised learning needs more compute resources while unsupervised learning runs on minimal hardware
e: Correct (A): Supervised has labeled inputs and outputs (learn mapping). Unsupervised has no labels (discover structure). A is wrong: N/A - this is correct. B is wrong: Both can use neural networks (autoencoders are unsupervised neural nets) and both can use statistical methods. C is wrong: Both are used in production (clustering for customer segmentation is unsupervised in production). D is wrong: Resource needs depend on data size and model complexity, not supervised vs unsupervised.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A company deployed a model 6 months ago. Performance has degraded despite no code changes. Most likely cause?
o: The model's hyperparameters have automatically adjusted themselves over time without authorization
Data drift where real-world data distributions have changed from what the model was trained on
The hardware running the model has physically degraded causing computational errors in predictions
The model file has become corrupted on disk due to repeated read operations during inference
e: Correct (B): Data drift is the most common cause of model degradation over time - user behavior, market conditions, or data sources change from training distribution. A is wrong: Hyperparameters are static after training - they don't auto-adjust in production unless explicitly programmed to do so. C is wrong: Hardware doesn't gradually degrade prediction quality - it either works or fails completely (bit-flip errors are extremely rare). D is wrong: Model files are read-only during inference - repeated reads don't corrupt files on modern storage systems.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which evaluation approach is BEST for a small dataset where every sample matters?
o: Single 80/20 train-test split to maximize training data while reserving evaluation samples
K-fold cross-validation that trains and evaluates on all data by rotating through multiple splits
Using the full dataset for training and evaluating on the same data to maximize signal
Random sampling of 10% of data for a quick spot-check of model performance
e: Correct (B): K-fold uses all data for both training and evaluation across k iterations, giving robust estimates with small datasets. A is wrong: A single split with small data gives high-variance estimates and wastes samples. C is wrong: Evaluating on training data gives overly optimistic results (doesn't measure generalization). D is wrong: 10% of a small dataset is too few samples for reliable evaluation metrics.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A retail company wants to automatically group products into categories without predefined labels. Which algorithm type?
o: Decision tree that recursively splits data using labeled examples to classify into known categories
K-means clustering that partitions data into groups by minimizing within-cluster distance
Linear regression that fits a line through data points to predict continuous numerical values
Gradient descent that iteratively optimizes model weights by following the loss function slope
e: Correct (B): K-means is an unsupervised clustering algorithm that groups data points by similarity without needing predefined labels. A is wrong: Decision trees need labeled data (supervised) and require predefined categories to classify into. C is wrong: Linear regression predicts numbers, it doesn't group items into categories. D is wrong: Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used during training, not a standalone grouping/classification method.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is the primary purpose of a confusion matrix in classification?
o: To visualize the optimization path of gradient descent during model training iterations
To show true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for evaluating classifier performance
To display the correlation between input features for identifying multicollinearity issues
To map the neural network architecture showing connections between layers and neurons
e: Correct (B): Confusion matrix shows TP/FP/TN/FN counts, revealing where the classifier succeeds and fails across all classes. A is wrong: Training optimization paths are shown with loss curves, not confusion matrices. C is wrong: Feature correlations are shown with correlation matrices or heatmaps, not confusion matrices. D is wrong: Network architecture is shown with computational graphs or architecture diagrams, not confusion matrices.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which AWS service should you use to add natural language understanding to an application without building custom models?
o: Amazon SageMaker AI for training a custom NLP transformer model from scratch on proprietary data
Amazon Comprehend which provides pre-trained NLP APIs for sentiment, entity, and language detection
Amazon EC2 instances configured with open-source NLP libraries for full customization control
AWS Glue which provides serverless ETL for transforming and cataloging structured data assets
e: Correct (B): Comprehend provides ready-to-use NLP APIs (sentiment, entities, key phrases, language) without any model building. A is wrong: SageMaker requires building/training custom models - the question asks for no custom model building. C is wrong: EC2 with NLP libraries means you're building and managing custom models yourself. D is wrong: Glue is for data ETL (extract, transform, load), not natural language understanding.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A model achieves 95% accuracy but only detects 20% of actual fraud cases. What should be prioritized?
o: Increasing overall accuracy further by adding more non-fraud training examples to the dataset
Improving recall (sensitivity) to catch more actual fraud cases even if some false positives increase
Reducing the model complexity to prevent overfitting on the fraud detection task
Increasing the prediction confidence threshold to ensure only high-certainty predictions are made
e: Correct (B): 20% fraud detection means 80% of fraud is missed (low recall). In fraud, missing real fraud is costly. Improving recall catches more true fraud. A is wrong: More non-fraud examples would further bias the model toward predicting legitimate, worsening fraud detection. C is wrong: The issue isn't overfitting (accuracy is high) - it's class imbalance causing low recall on the minority class. D is wrong: Higher threshold would make the model even more conservative, catching fewer fraud cases (lower recall).

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is the ML development lifecycle in correct order?
o: Deploy, Train, Collect Data, Evaluate, Define Problem, Feature Engineer
Define Problem, Collect Data, Feature Engineer, Train Model, Evaluate, Deploy and Monitor
Train Model, Define Problem, Feature Engineer, Collect Data, Deploy, Evaluate
Collect Data, Deploy, Define Problem, Train Model, Feature Engineer, Evaluate
e: Correct (B): The lifecycle flows: business problem definition, data collection, feature preparation, model training, evaluation against metrics, then deployment with monitoring. A is wrong: You cannot deploy before training, and problem definition must come before data collection. C is wrong: You cannot train before defining the problem or engineering features from collected data. D is wrong: Collection before problem definition means you don't know what to collect, and deploying before evaluation risks releasing a bad model.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A company needs to transcribe customer service calls to text. Which AWS service?
o: Amazon Polly which converts written text into natural-sounding speech audio output
Amazon Transcribe which converts speech audio into accurate text using automatic speech recognition
Amazon Translate which converts text from one natural language to another in real time
Amazon Textract which extracts structured text and data from scanned documents and images
e: Correct (B): Transcribe is speech-to-text (ASR) - converts audio recordings to written text. Purpose-built for call transcription. A is wrong: Polly is text-to-speech (the opposite direction) - it creates audio from text, not text from audio. C is wrong: Translate converts between languages (English to Spanish) - it doesn't convert speech to text. D is wrong: Textract extracts text from images/documents (OCR), not from audio recordings.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is labeled data and why is it important for supervised learning?
o: Data that has been encrypted with security labels to protect it during model training processes
Data with input-output pairs where correct answers are provided, enabling the model to learn the mapping
Data that has been tagged with metadata about its storage location and retention policy
Data that has been verified by quality assurance teams but does not contain target variable information
e: Correct (B): Labeled data has inputs (features) paired with correct outputs (targets). The model learns the input-output mapping by example. A is wrong: Security labels are access controls unrelated to ML - labeled data means having target/answer values attached. C is wrong: Storage metadata is data governance, not ML labels - labels are the correct answers for training. D is wrong: QA verification without target variables is still unlabeled for ML purposes - you need the actual answer values.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A model needs to identify whether an email is spam or not spam. What type of task is this?
o: Binary classification that assigns each input to exactly one of two possible categorical outcomes
Multi-class classification that distributes emails across many possible category labels simultaneously
Regression that predicts a continuous spam probability score without any categorical threshold
Clustering that groups similar emails together based on content patterns without predetermined labels
e: Correct (A): Spam/not-spam has exactly two classes, making it binary classification. Each email gets one label. B is wrong: Multi-class means 3+ categories (spam, promotions, social, primary) - two classes is specifically binary. C is wrong: While models may output probabilities internally, the task itself is categorical (spam or not) not continuous prediction. D is wrong: Clustering is unsupervised and doesn't assign predetermined labels like spam/not-spam.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: Which technique helps prevent overfitting during neural network training?
o: Increasing model complexity by adding more layers and neurons to capture all training patterns
Dropout regularization that randomly deactivates neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation
Removing the validation set to allow the model to train on maximum available labeled data
Increasing the learning rate to move through the loss landscape faster and avoid local patterns
e: Correct (B): Dropout randomly disables neurons during training, forcing the network to learn redundant representations and preventing over-reliance on specific neurons. A is wrong: More complexity increases overfitting risk - the model gains more capacity to memorize training noise. C is wrong: Removing validation eliminates the ability to detect overfitting - you need validation to know when to stop. D is wrong: Higher learning rate causes unstable training and overshooting optima, it doesn't prevent overfitting.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: A company wants to personalize product recommendations. Which technique is most relevant?
o: Collaborative filtering that predicts preferences based on similar users or similar item interaction patterns
Principal Component Analysis that reduces the dimensionality of product feature vectors
Time series forecasting that predicts future inventory needs based on historical demand patterns
Anomaly detection that identifies unusual purchase patterns that deviate from normal behavior
e: Correct (A): Collaborative filtering (user-based or item-based) powers recommendation engines by finding users with similar tastes or items with similar ratings. B is wrong: PCA reduces dimensions but doesn't directly generate recommendations - it's a preprocessing step, not a recommendation algorithm. C is wrong: Time series forecasting predicts future values (demand), not personalized product suggestions for individual users. D is wrong: Anomaly detection flags outliers, it doesn't recommend products a user would enjoy.

d: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML
q: What is the purpose of a training pipeline in ML?
o: A physical hardware connection between GPU clusters that transfers model weights during training
An automated workflow that orchestrates data processing, model training, evaluation, and registration reproducibly
A marketing funnel that tracks how customers move from awareness to purchase conversion stages
A network configuration that routes inference requests from clients to the appropriate model endpoint
e: Correct (B): ML pipelines automate and orchestrate the end-to-end workflow (data prep, training, evaluation, registration) ensuring reproducibility and consistency. A is wrong: Pipeline in ML refers to software workflow orchestration, not physical hardware connections between machines. C is wrong: Marketing funnels are business concepts unrelated to ML model development workflows. D is wrong: Request routing to endpoints is inference infrastructure (load balancing), not training pipeline orchestration.

q: What is a foundation model?
o: A small, task-specific model trained from scratch on a narrow dataset for one particular use case
A large model pre-trained on broad data that can be adapted to many different downstream tasks
A database schema that forms the structural foundation for storing and querying training datasets
A hardware architecture specification that defines the physical foundation for model training clusters
e: Correct (B): Foundation models (GPT, Claude, Llama) are trained on massive diverse data and adapted to many tasks via fine-tuning or prompting. A is wrong: Foundation models are the opposite of narrow - they're broadly capable, not task-specific, and trained on diverse data. C is wrong: Database schemas store data but aren't models - foundation model refers to the AI model itself, not storage infrastructure. D is wrong: Hardware specs define compute infrastructure, not the model's learned capabilities and knowledge.

q: Which is a key limitation of large language models?
o: They cannot process text inputs longer than 50 words due to memory architecture constraints
They may generate confident-sounding but factually incorrect information known as hallucinations
They require retraining from scratch every time a user submits a new prompt for inference
They can only produce outputs in the same language as their training data was written in
e: Correct (B): Hallucination is a core LLM limitation - models generate fluent, confident text that may be factually wrong because they predict likely tokens, not verified facts. A is wrong: Modern LLMs handle thousands to millions of tokens - context windows are large, not 50 words. C is wrong: Inference uses the already-trained model - no retraining happens per prompt. D is wrong: Multilingual models (many foundation models) can output in languages beyond their primary training language.

q: What is the primary function of Amazon Bedrock?
o: A managed Kubernetes service for deploying containerized ML model training workloads at scale
A unified API service providing access to multiple foundation models from different providers without managing infrastructure
A data warehouse optimized for storing and querying large-scale model training datasets efficiently
A CI/CD pipeline service for automating the testing and deployment of custom machine learning code
e: Correct (B): Bedrock provides API access to FMs from Anthropic, Meta, Amazon, and others - serverless, no infrastructure management needed. A is wrong: EKS is the Kubernetes service - Bedrock is specifically for accessing foundation models via API. C is wrong: Redshift is the data warehouse - Bedrock serves model inference, not data storage. D is wrong: CodePipeline/CodeBuild handle CI/CD - Bedrock provides model access, not deployment automation.

q: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?
o: A technique that retrains the foundation model weights using retrieved documents as new training data
A pattern that retrieves relevant external documents and provides them as context to the model before generation
A method of generating synthetic training data by randomly augmenting existing labeled examples
A compression algorithm that reduces model size by retrieving only the most important neural network weights
e: Correct (B): RAG retrieves relevant docs from a knowledge base and adds them to the prompt context, grounding generation in factual sources without retraining. A is wrong: RAG doesn't retrain model weights - it adds context at inference time. The model parameters stay frozen. C is wrong: Data augmentation creates training variations, but RAG operates at inference time to ground responses in external knowledge. D is wrong: Model compression (pruning/quantization) reduces parameters - RAG is about adding knowledge context, not reducing model size.

q: What differentiates generative AI from traditional discriminative AI models?
o: Generative AI creates new content (text, images, code) while discriminative models classify or predict from existing inputs
Generative AI only works with unstructured data while discriminative models only work with structured tabular data
Generative AI requires no training data while discriminative models need millions of labeled examples
Generative AI runs only on quantum computers while discriminative models run on classical hardware
e: Correct (A): Generative creates new outputs (text, images). Discriminative classifies/predicts boundaries between existing categories. Different objectives. B is wrong: Both can work with various data types - discriminative models handle images (CNNs) and text (BERT for classification). C is wrong: Generative AI requires massive training data (trillions of tokens for LLMs). D is wrong: Both run on classical GPU hardware - quantum computers aren't required or used for current generative AI.

q: A company wants to use a foundation model but ground its responses in company-specific documentation. Which approach?
o: Fine-tune the model on the entire internet to ensure it has comprehensive general knowledge
Implement RAG with a knowledge base containing company documents indexed for semantic retrieval
Increase the model temperature to maximum so it explores more possible answer spaces
Remove the model's safety guardrails so it can access all internal information without restrictions
e: Correct (B): RAG with indexed company docs grounds responses in your specific knowledge without retraining. Bedrock Knowledge Bases implements this. A is wrong: Training on the internet gives general knowledge, not company-specific answers - and you'd need your own docs specifically. C is wrong: Higher temperature increases randomness/creativity, it doesn't add knowledge the model doesn't have. D is wrong: Safety guardrails control harmful outputs, they don't restrict access to internal knowledge - that's a retrieval problem.

q: What is the role of tokens in large language models?
o: Tokens are authentication credentials that authorize users to access the model's API endpoints securely
Tokens are the basic units of text that models process, representing words, subwords, or characters
Tokens are virtual currency units used to pay for model inference compute costs per request
Tokens are encrypted data packets that protect sensitive information during model input processing
e: Correct (B): LLMs break text into tokens (words/subwords/characters) as their basic processing units. Both input and output are measured in tokens. A is wrong: API authentication uses API keys or IAM credentials, not the tokens that models process linguistically. C is wrong: While pricing is often per-token, the tokens themselves are linguistic units, not currency. D is wrong: Tokens aren't encryption - they're the segmented text units the model reads and generates.

q: What does model temperature control in text generation?
o: The physical cooling system temperature of GPU hardware running model inference workloads
The randomness of output where higher temperature increases creativity and lower temperature increases determinism
The speed at which the model generates tokens, with higher temperature producing faster output
The maximum context window size that determines how much input text the model can process
e: Correct (B): Temperature scales the probability distribution. Low temp (0-0.3) = deterministic/focused. High temp (0.7-1.0) = creative/diverse. A is wrong: Temperature is a software parameter in the sampling algorithm, not physical hardware cooling. C is wrong: Temperature doesn't affect generation speed - it affects output randomness/diversity. D is wrong: Context window is fixed by model architecture, not adjustable via temperature parameter.

q: Which is an example of a generative AI use case in business?
o: A rule-based calculator that computes employee payroll deductions using fixed tax formulas
An AI assistant that drafts customer email responses personalized to their inquiry and history
A database query that retrieves customer records matching a specific account number filter
A scheduled batch job that archives old log files to cold storage after 90 days retention
e: Correct (B): Drafting personalized email responses requires generating new, contextual text - a core generative AI capability. A is wrong: Fixed-formula calculations are deterministic rules, no AI or generation involved. C is wrong: Database queries retrieve existing data, they don't generate new content. D is wrong: Scheduled archival is infrastructure automation with fixed rules, not AI-generated content.

q: What is fine-tuning in the context of foundation models?
o: Adjusting hardware configurations like GPU memory allocation and CPU thread counts for inference
Further training a pre-trained model on domain-specific data to improve performance on targeted tasks
Modifying the model's API rate limits and throttling settings for production traffic management
Changing the network firewall rules that control which users can access the model endpoint
e: Correct (B): Fine-tuning continues training a pre-trained FM on your specific domain data, adapting it for your use case while leveraging pre-existing knowledge. A is wrong: Hardware configuration is infrastructure tuning, not model fine-tuning - it doesn't change model behavior or knowledge. C is wrong: Rate limits are API management settings, not model capability adjustments. D is wrong: Firewall rules are network security controls unrelated to model performance or knowledge.

q: What is an embedding in the context of AI?
o: A numerical vector representation that captures semantic meaning of text, images, or other data in continuous space
A physical chip embedded in hardware that accelerates specific model inference operations
A watermark embedded in generated content to identify AI-produced text and images
A malicious code injection embedded in prompts designed to manipulate model behavior
e: Correct (A): Embeddings map data to dense vectors where semantic similarity corresponds to vector proximity. Used in search, RAG, clustering. B is wrong: Hardware accelerators (TPUs, inference chips) are physical components, not the mathematical representations called embeddings. C is wrong: Watermarking is a separate technique for provenance tracking, not the vector representations used for semantic search. D is wrong: Prompt injection is a security attack, not the mathematical representation of semantic meaning.

q: A company wants to build a chatbot using a foundation model that answers only from approved knowledge. What prevents hallucination?
o: Setting temperature to maximum to ensure the model explores all possible answer combinations thoroughly
Implementing guardrails that detect off-topic responses and RAG to ground answers in verified sources
Removing all system prompts so the model responds purely from its pre-training knowledge base
Training the model on intentionally incorrect data so it learns to identify and avoid false statements
e: Correct (B): Guardrails filter inappropriate responses. RAG grounds answers in retrieved verified documents. Together they minimize hallucination. A is wrong: Max temperature increases randomness and makes hallucination worse, not better. C is wrong: Removing system prompts removes behavioral guidance - you'd lose control over response boundaries. D is wrong: Training on incorrect data would teach the model to generate incorrect information, making hallucination worse.

q: Which describes a key advantage of using managed foundation model services like Amazon Bedrock?
o: You must provision and manage GPU clusters yourself but get a discount on compute pricing
No infrastructure management required, with access to multiple provider models through a single API
You get exclusive permanent ownership of the foundation model weights for unlimited modification
The service guarantees 100% factual accuracy in all model outputs without any hallucination risk
e: Correct (B): Bedrock is serverless - no infrastructure to manage. Single API for Claude, Llama, Titan, etc. Focus on building, not ops. A is wrong: Bedrock is serverless - no GPU provisioning needed. That's the whole point of a managed service. C is wrong: You access models via API, you don't own the weights. Model providers retain ownership. D is wrong: No service guarantees zero hallucination - it's an inherent limitation of current LLMs.

q: What is a prompt in the context of generative AI?
o: The initial hardware boot sequence that loads model weights from storage into GPU memory
The input text or instructions given to a model that guides what output it should generate
The marketing material used to promote AI services to potential enterprise customers
The legal disclaimer displayed before users interact with any AI-powered system
e: Correct (B): A prompt is the user input (instructions, questions, context) that tells the model what to generate. Prompt design directly affects output quality. A is wrong: Hardware boot sequences are infrastructure operations, not the input that guides model generation. C is wrong: Marketing materials are business communications unrelated to model interaction mechanics. D is wrong: Legal disclaimers are compliance requirements, not the technical input mechanism for model generation.

q: What is the difference between pre-training and fine-tuning a foundation model?
o: Pre-training uses labeled data for a specific task while fine-tuning uses unlabeled general data
Pre-training learns general knowledge from massive diverse data while fine-tuning adapts to specific domains with smaller targeted datasets
Pre-training happens after deployment while fine-tuning happens before the model is first created
Pre-training takes minutes while fine-tuning requires months of compute on large GPU clusters
e: Correct (B): Pre-training builds broad capabilities from massive data (months, millions of dollars). Fine-tuning specializes with smaller domain data (hours to days). A is wrong: The reverse is true - pre-training uses massive (often unlabeled) data, fine-tuning uses smaller labeled domain data. C is wrong: Pre-training creates the initial model before any deployment. Fine-tuning happens after pre-training. D is wrong: Pre-training takes weeks/months on large clusters. Fine-tuning is the shorter process (hours to days).

q: Which prompt engineering technique provides examples of desired input-output pairs before asking the model to complete a new task?
o: Zero-shot prompting that gives instructions only without any examples of the expected format
Few-shot prompting that includes several input-output examples before the actual task request
Chain-of-thought prompting that asks the model to explain its reasoning step by step
System prompting that defines the model's persona and behavioral boundaries for the session
e: Correct (B): Few-shot provides examples (2-5 typically) showing the desired pattern, then asks the model to follow that pattern for new input. A is wrong: Zero-shot gives only instructions with no examples - it relies on the model understanding from description alone. C is wrong: Chain-of-thought asks for reasoning steps, not input-output examples - it's about showing work, not demonstrating format. D is wrong: System prompts set behavior/persona, they don't provide task examples for pattern matching.

q: A developer wants the model to reason through a complex math problem before giving the final answer. Which technique?
o: Few-shot prompting that shows multiple solved examples for the model to mimic output format
Chain-of-thought prompting that instructs the model to show intermediate reasoning steps explicitly
Temperature reduction that makes the model more deterministic in selecting the most probable tokens
Increasing max tokens to give the model more output space for a longer final answer
e: Correct (B): Chain-of-thought ("Let's think step by step") makes the model show work, improving accuracy on multi-step reasoning tasks. A is wrong: Few-shot shows examples but doesn't specifically instruct step-by-step reasoning through the problem. C is wrong: Lower temperature increases determinism but doesn't make the model show its reasoning process. D is wrong: More output tokens gives space but doesn't instruct the model to reason through intermediate steps.

q: What is the purpose of a system prompt in foundation model interactions?
o: To encrypt the user's input data before it reaches the model for privacy protection
To define the model's role, behavior constraints, and response guidelines for the entire session
To specify the hardware configuration that the model should use for processing the request
To authenticate the user's identity and verify they have permission to access the model
e: Correct (B): System prompts set persona, rules, format requirements, and behavioral boundaries that persist across the conversation session. A is wrong: Encryption happens at the transport layer (TLS), not in the prompt - system prompts are instructions, not security mechanisms. C is wrong: Hardware allocation is managed by the service infrastructure, not controlled by prompt content. D is wrong: Authentication uses API keys or IAM, not the system prompt - prompts instruct behavior, not access control.

q: A company fine-tuned a model on customer support data. The model now gives excellent support answers but has lost general knowledge. What happened?
o: The model's API key expired causing it to fall back to a limited capability default mode
Catastrophic forgetting where fine-tuning on narrow data overwrote previously learned general capabilities
The training data was too large causing memory overflow that corrupted the original model weights
A competitor attacked the model with adversarial prompts that erased its general knowledge base
e: Correct (B): Catastrophic forgetting occurs when fine-tuning on narrow domain data overwrites general knowledge. Common with aggressive fine-tuning. A is wrong: API key expiration would block access entirely, not selectively degrade knowledge areas. C is wrong: Memory overflow would cause training failure/crash, not selective knowledge loss. D is wrong: Adversarial prompts affect individual responses, they can't permanently erase model weights or training.

q: Which approach allows using a foundation model with company data WITHOUT modifying model weights?
o: Full fine-tuning that retrains all model layers on company-specific labeled datasets
RAG that retrieves company documents at inference time and includes them in the prompt context
Continued pre-training that extends the model's knowledge by training on additional company corpus
LoRA that adds trainable low-rank matrices to modify model behavior through weight updates
e: Correct (B): RAG adds external knowledge at inference time via retrieval - model weights stay frozen. No training required. A is wrong: Full fine-tuning explicitly modifies all model weights through gradient updates. C is wrong: Continued pre-training updates model weights with new data - it changes the model parameters. D is wrong: LoRA adds and trains new weight matrices, modifying the model's effective parameters.

q: What is prompt injection and how can it be mitigated?
o: A technique to compress prompts for faster processing, mitigated by increasing context window size
A malicious attempt to override system instructions through crafted user input, mitigated by input validation and guardrails
A method to improve model accuracy by injecting additional training examples into the prompt
A hardware vulnerability where electrical signals interfere with model processing on the GPU
e: Correct (B): Prompt injection tricks the model into ignoring system instructions (e.g., 'ignore previous instructions'). Mitigate with input filtering, guardrails, and output validation. A is wrong: Prompt compression is an efficiency technique, not a security threat. C is wrong: Adding examples to prompts is few-shot learning, a legitimate technique - not a security vulnerability. D is wrong: Hardware signal interference is an electrical engineering problem unrelated to AI prompt security.

q: A model generates valid but potentially harmful instructions when asked. Which AWS service adds safety controls?
o: Amazon CloudWatch which monitors infrastructure metrics and application performance logs
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails which filters harmful content, PII, and enforces topic boundaries on model inputs and outputs
AWS WAF which blocks malicious HTTP requests at the network edge before reaching application servers
Amazon Inspector which scans EC2 instances and container images for software vulnerabilities
e: Correct (B): Bedrock Guardrails applies content filters, denied topics, PII redaction, and word blocks on both inputs and outputs. A is wrong: CloudWatch monitors system metrics and logs, it doesn't filter or control model content. C is wrong: WAF filters HTTP traffic patterns (SQL injection, XSS), not AI-generated content semantics. D is wrong: Inspector scans for software CVEs on infrastructure, not harmful content in model responses.

q: When evaluating a foundation model for a text summarization task, which metric is MOST relevant?
o: Frames per second which measures how quickly the model can process visual input data
ROUGE score which measures overlap between generated summaries and reference human-written summaries
Disk IOPS which measures the storage throughput available for loading model weight files
Mean time between failures which measures hardware reliability of the inference infrastructure
e: Correct (B): ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) compares generated text against reference summaries using n-gram overlap. Standard for summarization. A is wrong: FPS is a video/image processing metric, irrelevant to text summarization quality. C is wrong: IOPS is a storage performance metric for infrastructure, not model output quality. D is wrong: MTBF measures hardware reliability, not the quality of generated text summaries.

q: What is the benefit of using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases for RAG instead of building your own retrieval pipeline?
o: It retrains the foundation model weights on your documents for permanent knowledge integration
It manages document ingestion, chunking, embedding, vector storage, and retrieval as a fully managed service
It eliminates the need for any source documents since the service generates knowledge automatically
It guarantees zero hallucination in all model responses regardless of question complexity
e: Correct (B): Knowledge Bases handles the entire RAG pipeline (ingest, chunk, embed, store, retrieve) without you building each component. Fully managed. A is wrong: Knowledge Bases doesn't retrain model weights - it retrieves documents at inference time. The model stays frozen. C is wrong: You must provide source documents - the service indexes YOUR content, it doesn't generate knowledge from nothing. D is wrong: RAG reduces hallucination but cannot eliminate it entirely - the model may still hallucinate despite retrieved context.

q: A developer needs the model to output responses in a strict JSON format. Which technique works best?
o: Increasing temperature to explore more creative structural variations of the output format
Providing explicit format instructions with JSON examples in the prompt and using structured output parsing
Reducing max tokens so the model has less space and must compress into structured format
Using a larger model which automatically knows to respond in JSON without any instructions
e: Correct (B): Explicit format instructions ("Respond in JSON with these keys...") plus examples in the prompt, combined with output parsers, ensures consistent JSON. A is wrong: Higher temperature increases randomness, making format adherence less reliable, not more. C is wrong: Reducing tokens truncates output, potentially breaking JSON structure mid-response. D is wrong: Larger models don't automatically use JSON - they still need instructions. Size doesn't determine output format.

q: What is the difference between zero-shot and few-shot prompting?
o: Zero-shot uses a small model and few-shot uses a large model for the same task
Zero-shot provides no examples while few-shot provides several examples of the desired input-output pattern
Zero-shot works offline without internet while few-shot requires real-time API connectivity
Zero-shot processes images only while few-shot processes text only as input modalities
e: Correct (B): Zero-shot: instructions only, no examples (relies on model understanding). Few-shot: includes examples showing the pattern before the actual task. A is wrong: Both techniques work with any model size - the difference is prompt content, not model scale. C is wrong: Both are prompting strategies that work identically whether online or offline. D is wrong: Both can handle any modality the model supports - the difference is whether examples are provided.

q: A company wants to build an AI agent that can search databases, call APIs, and take actions. Which AWS service?
o: Amazon Comprehend which provides pre-built NLP APIs for analyzing text sentiment and entities
Amazon Bedrock Agents which orchestrate foundation models with tools, APIs, and knowledge bases to complete multi-step tasks
Amazon Kinesis which ingests and processes real-time streaming data from multiple sources
Amazon SQS which queues messages between distributed application components for decoupling
e: Correct (B): Bedrock Agents connect FMs to tools (APIs, databases, Lambda functions) and orchestrate multi-step reasoning to complete tasks autonomously. A is wrong: Comprehend analyzes text (sentiment, entities) but cannot take actions, call APIs, or orchestrate multi-step workflows. C is wrong: Kinesis processes streaming data (logs, events) - it doesn't orchestrate AI decision-making or tool use. D is wrong: SQS is a message queue for decoupling services, not an AI orchestration layer.

q: Which factor MOST affects foundation model inference cost?
o: The programming language used to write the application code that calls the model API
The number of input and output tokens processed per request multiplied by the model's per-token price
The geographic region where the developer's laptop is physically located during development
The color scheme and styling of the user interface that displays the model's generated responses
e: Correct (B): FM pricing is primarily per-token (input + output). More tokens = higher cost. Larger models charge more per token. A is wrong: Application language doesn't affect model pricing - the model API accepts the same request regardless of calling language. C is wrong: Developer location doesn't affect pricing - AWS region of the endpoint matters for latency but pricing is standard. D is wrong: UI styling is frontend presentation with zero impact on model inference cost.

q: A model produces different outputs each time for the same prompt. How to make it deterministic?
o: Set temperature to 0 (or near 0) to select the highest probability token at each step
Set temperature to maximum to ensure full exploration of all possible token combinations
Remove the system prompt so the model has fewer instructions influencing its behavior
Shorten the prompt to reduce the number of variables the model needs to consider
e: Correct (A): Temperature 0 means greedy decoding - always picks the most probable next token. Same input = same output. B is wrong: Max temperature maximizes randomness, producing different outputs every time. C is wrong: Removing system prompts doesn't affect randomness - temperature controls the sampling distribution. D is wrong: Shorter prompts don't reduce randomness - temperature is the control mechanism for output variability.

q: What is the purpose of chunking in a RAG pipeline?
o: Splitting the foundation model into smaller pieces that run on multiple GPUs in parallel
Breaking documents into smaller segments so they can be individually embedded and retrieved with precision
Compressing model weights into smaller files for faster download from the model registry
Dividing user prompts into fragments that are processed by different model instances simultaneously
e: Correct (B): Chunking splits documents into smaller pieces (paragraphs, sections) so embeddings capture specific content and retrieval returns relevant segments, not entire documents. A is wrong: Model parallelism splits model layers across GPUs - that's distributed inference, not document processing. C is wrong: Weight compression is quantization/pruning - unrelated to document preparation for retrieval. D is wrong: Prompt processing happens in one model instance - chunking refers to source documents, not user prompts.

q: Which evaluation metric measures whether a generative model's output is factually consistent with source documents?
o: BLEU score which measures n-gram precision overlap between generated and reference translations
Faithfulness/groundedness score which assesses whether generated claims are supported by provided source material
Perplexity which measures how surprised the model is by the next token in a sequence
Latency which measures the time elapsed between sending a request and receiving the model's response
e: Correct (B): Faithfulness/groundedness evaluates whether the model's claims are actually supported by the retrieved source documents - critical for RAG evaluation. A is wrong: BLEU measures translation quality via n-gram overlap with references, not factual consistency with source documents. C is wrong: Perplexity measures model confidence/fluency, not factual accuracy against source material. D is wrong: Latency is a performance metric (speed), not a quality metric for factual consistency.

q: A model needs to process both an image and text question about that image. What capability is required?
o: Single-modal processing that handles text input and generates text output exclusively
Multimodal capability that can accept and reason across multiple input types like text and images together
Audio transcription that converts spoken words into written text for the model to process
Batch inference that processes many requests simultaneously for high throughput efficiency
e: Correct (B): Multimodal models (like Claude with vision) accept multiple input types (text + images) and reason across them jointly. A is wrong: Single-modal (text-only) cannot process images - it's limited to one input type. C is wrong: Audio transcription converts speech to text - it doesn't enable image understanding. D is wrong: Batch inference is about throughput (many requests), not input modality capability.

q: What is model distillation?
o: Removing a model from production and archiving its weights for compliance record-keeping purposes
Training a smaller student model to replicate a larger teacher model's behavior, reducing inference cost while preserving quality
Extracting training data from a model by repeatedly querying it with adversarial prompts
Combining multiple different model architectures into a single unified model through weight merging
e: Correct (B): Distillation trains a smaller model to mimic the larger model's outputs. Result: cheaper inference, lower latency, similar quality. A is wrong: Archiving is storage/compliance, not a training technique. The model isn't being learned from. C is wrong: Data extraction attacks are a security concern, not a legitimate training methodology. D is wrong: Model merging combines weights directly, while distillation trains a new smaller model on the teacher's outputs.

q: A user wants to limit a model's responses to only topics about AWS services. Which approach?
o: Train the model exclusively on AWS documentation and delete all other knowledge from its weights
Configure denied topic guardrails in Amazon Bedrock that block responses outside approved subject areas
Set temperature to 0 which restricts the model to only factual technical topics by default
Use a smaller model which has limited knowledge and therefore cannot discuss non-AWS topics
e: Correct (B): Bedrock Guardrails denied topics feature blocks responses outside configured subject areas. Programmatic enforcement. A is wrong: You cannot selectively delete knowledge from model weights - fine-tuning adds knowledge but can't surgically remove topics. C is wrong: Temperature 0 affects randomness, not topic restriction. The model can discuss any topic at any temperature. D is wrong: Smaller models still have broad knowledge - size doesn't limit topic coverage to specific domains.

q: What is the purpose of an embedding model in a RAG architecture?
o: To generate the final natural language response that the user sees in the chat interface
To convert text chunks into numerical vectors that enable semantic similarity search in a vector database
To authenticate users before they can submit queries to the retrieval system
To compress documents into smaller file sizes for efficient storage in the knowledge base
e: Correct (B): Embedding models convert text to dense vectors. Similar meanings get similar vectors. This enables semantic search (find relevant chunks by meaning, not keywords). A is wrong: The generation model (LLM) produces the final response - the embedding model only creates searchable vectors. C is wrong: Authentication is handled by IAM/API keys, not embedding models. D is wrong: Embeddings don't compress files for storage - they create mathematical representations for similarity search.

q: When should you choose fine-tuning over RAG for customizing a foundation model?
o: When you need the model to adopt a specific style, tone, or domain vocabulary consistently across all interactions
When you need real-time access to frequently changing documents that are updated daily
When you want to avoid any additional cost beyond the base model inference pricing
When you need to restrict the model from accessing any external data sources during inference
e: Correct (A): Fine-tuning changes model behavior/style permanently. Best for consistent tone, domain terminology, or format adherence across all responses. B is wrong: RAG is better for frequently changing documents - fine-tuning requires retraining whenever content updates. C is wrong: Fine-tuning has significant training costs. RAG also adds cost (retrieval + longer prompts). Neither is free. D is wrong: Fine-tuning doesn't restrict external access - that's a system architecture decision independent of fine-tuning vs RAG.

q: What is the primary purpose of responsible AI practices?
o: To maximize model accuracy at any cost by removing all constraints on model training and deployment
To ensure AI systems are fair, transparent, safe, and accountable while minimizing potential harms
To reduce AI development costs by skipping testing and evaluation phases for faster time to market
To restrict AI usage to only government-approved applications through mandatory licensing
e: Correct (B): Responsible AI ensures systems work equitably, transparently, safely, and accountably - balancing capability with harm mitigation. A is wrong: Responsible AI explicitly adds constraints (fairness, safety) rather than removing them - accuracy alone isn't the goal. C is wrong: Responsible AI adds evaluation steps (bias testing, safety checks), it doesn't skip them for speed. D is wrong: Responsible AI is about ethical principles and practices, not government licensing restrictions.

q: A model trained on historical hiring data consistently rejects female applicants for engineering roles. What type of issue is this?
o: Model underfitting where the algorithm is too simple to understand nuanced qualification criteria
Algorithmic bias where historical discrimination patterns in training data are learned and perpetuated by the model
Data drift where applicant demographics have shifted since the model was originally trained
Feature leakage where interview results were accidentally included in the training feature set
e: Correct (B): Historical hiring data reflects past discrimination. The model learns these biased patterns and perpetuates them against protected groups. A is wrong: Underfitting means poor performance on all predictions, not systematic discrimination against one group. C is wrong: Drift is temporal distribution change - the bias exists from day one because the training data embeds historical discrimination. D is wrong: Feature leakage inflates overall accuracy, it doesn't cause systematic gender-based discrimination.

q: What does model explainability mean and why is it important?
o: The ability to explain the model's source code to new developers joining the engineering team
The ability to understand and communicate why a model made a specific prediction or decision
The ability to explain the cloud infrastructure costs associated with running the model in production
The ability to describe the marketing strategy for selling AI products to enterprise customers
e: Correct (B): Explainability means understanding why the model produced a specific output - critical for trust, debugging, compliance, and accountability. A is wrong: Code readability is software engineering practice, not AI explainability about model decisions. C is wrong: Cost explanation is financial management, not model behavior interpretation. D is wrong: Marketing strategy is business development, unrelated to understanding model decision-making.

q: A company deploys an AI system that makes lending decisions. Which responsible AI principle requires them to explain why an applicant was denied?
o: Scalability which requires the system to handle increasing application volumes efficiently
Transparency and explainability which requires clear communication of decision factors to affected individuals
Performance optimization which requires the model to process decisions within latency thresholds
Cost efficiency which requires minimizing the compute resources used per lending decision
e: Correct (B): Transparency/explainability requires that people affected by AI decisions can understand why - especially in regulated domains like lending (also legally required). A is wrong: Scalability is about handling volume, not explaining individual decisions to applicants. C is wrong: Performance/latency is about speed, not providing reasons for decisions. D is wrong: Cost efficiency is about resource usage, not communication of decision rationale.

q: How can you detect bias in a foundation model's outputs?
o: By checking if the model processes requests quickly, since biased models are always slower
By testing with diverse demographic scenarios and measuring whether outputs differ systematically across protected groups
By counting the total number of parameters in the model, since larger models are always less biased
By verifying the model was trained in a specific geographic region known for producing unbiased data
e: Correct (B): Bias testing sends equivalent prompts varying only demographic attributes, measuring whether outputs differ unfairly across gender, race, age, etc. A is wrong: Processing speed has no relationship to bias - biased and unbiased models can be equally fast. C is wrong: Model size doesn't determine bias - large models can be biased and small models can be fair. D is wrong: Training region doesn't determine bias - bias comes from data content and representation, not geography.

q: What is the purpose of human-in-the-loop in AI systems?
o: To require human approval at critical decision points, providing oversight and catching errors before actions are taken
To have humans manually type all model outputs because AI cannot generate text independently
To replace the AI system entirely with human workers who manually process all requests
To ensure humans physically maintain the server hardware by monitoring temperature and power consumption
e: Correct (A): Human-in-the-loop places human oversight at critical points - review, approve, or override AI decisions. Balances automation with accountability. B is wrong: AI generates text independently - human-in-the-loop means oversight at key decisions, not typing everything manually. C is wrong: HITL augments AI with human oversight, it doesn't replace AI entirely. D is wrong: Hardware maintenance is operations work, not the AI governance concept of human oversight on model decisions.

q: A company's AI chatbot occasionally generates harmful content. Which AWS service helps prevent this?
o: Amazon S3 bucket policies which control access to stored objects based on IAM principals
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails which applies content filters to block harmful, inappropriate, or off-topic model outputs
AWS CloudFormation which automates infrastructure provisioning using declarative template definitions
Amazon RDS which provides managed relational database instances with automated backup and patching
e: Correct (B): Guardrails filters model inputs and outputs against content policies (hate speech, violence, PII, denied topics). Real-time blocking. A is wrong: S3 policies control object storage access, not AI content quality. C is wrong: CloudFormation provisions infrastructure, it doesn't filter AI-generated content. D is wrong: RDS manages databases, it has no content filtering or AI safety capabilities.

q: What is data privacy's role in responsible AI?
o: Data privacy means making all training data publicly available so anyone can verify the model is fair
Protecting personal information throughout the AI lifecycle through anonymization, access controls, and compliance with regulations
Data privacy means using only synthetic data so no real person's information ever enters any system
Data privacy means storing data exclusively in on-premises servers and never using any cloud services
e: Correct (B): Data privacy protects PII through anonymization, encryption, access controls, retention policies, and regulatory compliance (GDPR, CCPA) across the full AI lifecycle. A is wrong: Publishing training data violates privacy - privacy means protecting information, not exposing it. C is wrong: Synthetic-only data is impractical and not required - privacy means protecting real data appropriately when used. D is wrong: Cloud vs on-premises is an infrastructure choice - privacy is achieved through controls regardless of location.

q: Who is responsible for securing data sent to Amazon Bedrock foundation models?
o: The foundation model provider (Anthropic, Meta) is solely responsible for all customer data security
The customer is responsible for data sent to the service, while AWS secures the underlying infrastructure
AWS automatically encrypts and permanently stores all prompts for future model training improvement
No security is needed because foundation model APIs are inherently secure without any configuration
e: Correct (B): Shared responsibility model: customer secures their data, inputs, configurations. AWS secures the platform, compute, networking infrastructure. A is wrong: Model providers build the model, but once on Bedrock, AWS and customer share security responsibility - providers don't manage customer data. C is wrong: Bedrock doesn't use customer prompts for training by default. Prompts are not stored permanently without customer choosing logging. D is wrong: Configuration is always needed - IAM policies, encryption settings, VPC endpoints, logging must be configured by the customer.

q: How should sensitive PII be handled before sending to a foundation model?
o: Include all PII unmodified because foundation models are inherently secure and cannot leak information
Mask or redact PII before sending to the model using services like Bedrock Guardrails PII detection
Convert PII to uppercase to make it stand out so the model knows to treat it carefully
Store PII in a separate database that the model queries directly using administrator credentials
e: Correct (B): Mask/redact PII before it reaches the model. Bedrock Guardrails can auto-detect and redact PII in inputs and outputs. A is wrong: Models can repeat PII in outputs, be extracted via prompt attacks, or PII may appear in logs. Never send unnecessary PII. C is wrong: Capitalization doesn't protect data - the model still receives and may output the sensitive information. D is wrong: Direct database access with admin credentials creates massive security risk and doesn't protect PII from model exposure.

q: Which AWS service provides identity-based access control for Bedrock model invocations?
o: Amazon GuardDuty which monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized access attempts
AWS IAM which controls who can invoke which models through policies attached to users, roles, and groups
Amazon CloudFront which distributes content globally through edge locations for low latency
AWS Direct Connect which provides dedicated network connectivity from on-premises to AWS
e: Correct (B): IAM policies control bedrock:InvokeModel permissions. You specify which principals can call which model IDs. Fine-grained access control. A is wrong: GuardDuty detects threats after they happen - it monitors, it doesn't control access. C is wrong: CloudFront is a CDN for content delivery, not an access control service for AI models. D is wrong: Direct Connect is network connectivity, not identity-based access control.

q: A compliance team needs to log all foundation model interactions for audit purposes. Which service?
o: AWS CloudTrail which logs API calls and can capture model invocation details for audit trails
Amazon SageMaker Feature Store which stores and retrieves ML features for model training pipelines
AWS CodeCommit which stores source code in managed Git repositories for version control
Amazon ElastiCache which provides in-memory caching for reducing application read latency
e: Correct (A): CloudTrail logs all Bedrock API calls (InvokeModel, etc.). Bedrock also supports model invocation logging to S3/CloudWatch for full prompt/response capture. B is wrong: Feature Store manages ML features (training data), not audit logs of model interactions. C is wrong: CodeCommit stores code, not runtime model invocation logs. D is wrong: ElastiCache is for application caching, not audit logging.

q: What is the purpose of model governance in AI systems?
o: To increase model inference speed by removing governance overhead and compliance checkpoints
To establish policies, processes, and controls for responsible model development, deployment, and monitoring throughout the lifecycle
To transfer all legal liability for AI decisions from the company to the model provider
To prevent any model from being updated after initial deployment ensuring permanent consistency
e: Correct (B): Governance establishes oversight: who approves models, what testing is required, how monitoring works, who is accountable, what regulations apply. A is wrong: Governance adds necessary oversight - removing it for speed defeats the purpose of responsible AI. C is wrong: Liability cannot be transferred by governance - companies remain responsible for AI systems they deploy. D is wrong: Models need updates (bias fixes, drift correction) - governance manages change safely, not prevents it.

q: A company must ensure AI-generated content complies with industry regulations. Which approach?
o: Trust that the foundation model was trained to follow all regulations automatically without verification
Implement output guardrails, human review workflows, and automated compliance checks before content reaches end users
Deploy the model in a region without regulations to avoid all compliance requirements entirely
Only use AI internally and assume internal usage has no regulatory or compliance implications
e: Correct (B): Compliance requires guardrails filtering outputs, human review for high-risk content, and automated checks validating regulatory requirements before publication. A is wrong: Models have no inherent regulatory knowledge - they can generate non-compliant content without safeguards. C is wrong: Regulations often apply based on where users/data reside, not where compute runs. You cannot avoid compliance by region-shopping. D is wrong: Internal AI use still has compliance implications (employee data, financial decisions, HR decisions are all regulated).

q: How does Amazon Bedrock protect customer data by default?
o: Customer prompts are shared across all Bedrock users to improve model performance for everyone
Customer data is encrypted in transit and at rest, not used for model training, and isolated between customers
All customer data is stored permanently in public S3 buckets for transparency and reproducibility
Customer data is sent to foundation model providers for quality review and improvement
e: Correct (B): Bedrock encrypts data in transit (TLS) and at rest (KMS). Customer prompts are not used to train base models. Tenant isolation enforced. A is wrong: Bedrock has strict tenant isolation - customer data is never shared across accounts or used for other customers. C is wrong: Data is encrypted and access-controlled, never placed in public storage. D is wrong: Customer data stays within AWS - it's not sent to model providers like Anthropic or Meta.

q: What is the shared responsibility model for AI services on AWS?
o: AWS is responsible for everything including customer's data, prompts, and application logic
Customer manages their data, access policies, and application logic while AWS secures the infrastructure, platform, and model hosting
The foundation model provider alone handles all security for both infrastructure and customer data
Responsibility is assigned randomly per request to distribute risk evenly across all parties
e: Correct (B): Customer: data, IAM, guardrails, app logic, compliance. AWS: physical security, compute, networking, platform patching, model hosting infrastructure. A is wrong: AWS doesn't manage customer data or application logic - that's the customer's responsibility under shared responsibility. C is wrong: On Bedrock, AWS manages infrastructure where models run - providers built the model but AWS runs it and customer configures access. D is wrong: Responsibility is clearly defined and contractual, not random or variable.

q: Which practice ensures AI model outputs can be traced back to their inputs for accountability?
o: Deleting all logs immediately after inference to minimize storage costs and data retention risk
Implementing comprehensive logging of prompts, model parameters, and responses with correlation IDs
Disabling all monitoring to reduce system complexity and improve inference latency
Using anonymous model endpoints that cannot be traced to any specific team or application
e: Correct (B): Logging prompts, responses, model versions, and parameters with correlation IDs enables full audit trail. Required for accountability and debugging. A is wrong: Deleting logs destroys the audit trail needed for accountability, compliance, and incident investigation. C is wrong: Disabling monitoring eliminates visibility into system behavior - you cannot ensure accountability without observability. D is wrong: Anonymous endpoints prevent attribution - accountability requires knowing which system produced which output.

q: A team discovers their AI model produces biased outcomes in production. What is the correct governance response?
o: Ignore the bias since the model passed initial testing before deployment and is therefore certified fair
